speaker: interviewer
text: --March the 25th, 1996, Sydney, Australia. OK. Elizabeth, you were telling us about the news you knew of what was going on in the rest of Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course, we know. We knew, because we listened to the radio, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was so afraid. We was-- we-- we knows it-- it must be something. We will be never surviving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even then you felt that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think about leaving Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My brother was offered a job here in Australia, in Melbourne, and my mother said, don't leave us. We are a little family, you know? We was three together, like three little bird, you know? And she don't let him go. And maybe if she let him go, maybe it's-- it's a different life, a different things every day. Maybe he's not killed, and my mother-- but my mother don't want to separate the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did the family take any precautions at all? Did it-- did you start putting money aside?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have no money that time, because we using it up everything. We-- we don't earn any money, you know? Because they-- they take it away, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we don't survive, because when we go to the ghetto and we live what-- they take us our gold and everything. They-- you have to put it in, everything what you have. If you have any gold or-- or dollar, or things, you have to give it to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the authorities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So let's go back. You were notified that you have to go to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother received the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any thought that you wouldn't go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You can't. You have to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do? What was the next step?

speaker: interviewee
text: Have-- we pack up our things. And how I say? The-- we only take a bed to the ghetto. All-- all the other furnishing we put it in my father's place, because he has a big place. And the-- all the-- the linen things that are all-- everything-- the clothes, the-- the coats and things, we-- we put it in my father storage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elizabeth, what did your Christian friends say when they heard that you had to go to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said if you want, we can put some things away from you. That's how-- that's how my [NON-ENGLISH]-- my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tapestry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --tapestry-- it's still here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: It survived, because two, three, we give it to one of my friend, the other two, three another friend, and my brother's suit, and with one friend, and my mother fur jacket in the other friend, you know? So we-- we give it to the nice people who asking. They said, we put something away from you-- to-- to you. Maybe-- maybe you come back, and then you need it. And that's it. So that's what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got the news that you had to go to the ghetto, did you know where that area was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: The area was around the synagogue, you know? And the very poor--

speaker: interviewer
text: Area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Area, yes. But we was happy, because we was still alive. And we have our uncle with us, and my cousins with us, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a fence around the ghetto at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was no fence. But it was all lock up gate. And after 6:00, you can't go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who policed it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish police boys-- I-- police-- old men who was still there, because all the young people was in the arbeit camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were too young for the arbeit camp, or-- why didn't you have to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had I go? I-- I-- I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't stay with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only stay for few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we have to go move in in that factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so let's start back with the ghetto. You moved into the ghetto with your mother. Was there a cooking facility?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was one kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she share it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, with my-- my-- my uncle-- he-- my mother brother, and his wife, and his two daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the bedding was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in-- in-- yes. It was one bedroom, and was all the beds, [LAUGHS] and one kitchen, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Indoors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many people lived in the one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many? Two, four, six-- six of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they get food to cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. For how I say to you, we take something with us, what we get-- a little flour, a little fat things, you know? But how I said to you, my girlfriend mother bring a piece of bread, another girlfriend mother bring a piece of bread, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was free for people to come in and out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did they do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the dark-- and they-- they-- the-- with the-- just drop it in in the window, because you can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who-- if the Jewish police found them, would they have been in trouble from the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because the Jewish police have to report it to the authority.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I-- I don't know. I have no experience of that, you know? Because I was working. I have two job. Even then I still have two job, because I was working in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cotton factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And in the morning or when I was have free, I have a friend who has a salon in a-- Hungary, and he-- she gave me a job. So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sewing?

speaker: interviewee
text: --go a few hours for sewing, yes. So I earn a little bit of money, so we can buy some food. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the cotton factory that you worked at?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ujpest.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but near the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I have to walk about 1/2 an hour-- good 1/2 an hour to--

speaker: interviewer
text: By this stage did you have to have stars on you-- gold stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you have to. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, but I was-- I always have a-- a working jacket, and I wear the star on the working jacket. And when I wanted to go somewhere, I take it off, the jacket, and I go without the star. Because nobody think I am a Jewish girl, because I was fair. And so I-- I don't look a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of places are you talking about? Where would you have wanted to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: At maybe to see my girlfriend, you know, to talk to her, because she can't come to the ghetto, you know? So I was going into her place, that nobody should see me. But I can't go with the star, because the star straight away show who I am. And even sometimes we go to the movie. I take the star off, and we go to the movie. Because they want I should go with them. You know, it was-- for them, it was fun. For me, I go because they wanted I should go. But I was worried always. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: At this stage, did you still see your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At that time he-- he give us food. He give us some food. And that-- I-- I go to him. But even where I go to him daytime-- if I go to him, I can wear the star. But after 6 o'clock or something-- I don't remember-- 5 or 6 o'clock-- I had to take off the star, or I can't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: If your father was born a Jew, but he didn't have papers to go into the ghetto, he-- what-- didn't have to go into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because he was not a Jew anymore, and his wife was a-- a born Catholic. So he was a mixed marriage. He don't need to go to the ghetto. And that's why he was thinking if nothing will happen to him. But unfortunately, it is happen to him. And when that Hungarian [NON-ENGLISH] come to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Power.

speaker: interviewee
text: --power, yes, they take Miklos Horthy off, and that-- then they take him away and kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to the movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember films that you may have seen then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. The-- [LAUGHS] the Hungarian films there, because no American film coming anymore, not overseas films; and, of course, the all operettes, and things, you know? We-- we loved to see it. And we went with my girlfriends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you a movie buff before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, because my mother cousins had three movie theater in Ujpest. It was a wealthy family, and I don't need to pay. [LAUGHS] So we go-- I take my girlfriends, and we go to the movies. And where a new film come, we was there-- the first to see it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- do you remember what movies you saw? Were they American movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Al Jolson, the and Sonja Henie with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ice--

speaker: interviewee
text: --ice skating. I love it. We love it; and that was big things; and Spencer Tracy. And oh, my sweetheart was Nelson Eddy. We even put our pocket money together and send him a letter to send us a photo. So we 10 girls, we get one photo from Eddy Nelson. And we was so happy. [LAUGHS] We have a photo, and we keep it in school. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody should see it, and-- You also met Josephine Baker once, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because she was my father girlfriend in-- in Budapest. It's cost him a lot of money. Can you imagine? For a little Jewish Ujpest-- Ujpest Jewish boy have a girlfriend, Josephine Baker-- she don't do it for nothing. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. That's-- So you went to the movies even though you were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time yes, because that time was not anymore belongs to my uncles, you know? In the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the same theater you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who owned it then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who owned it then? Non-Jewish people, you know? They-- they just take it away, and give it to somebody else. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to that uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Never come home. My mother have 35 cousins, and not one survived-- 35. Because my great-grandmother have two big flat house, and every month, my mother went to get the money. So all the cousins come together in one of my auntie's place. And it was 35 cousins-- and not one coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Tell us about your work in the cotton factory. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I make-- I-- working in the woven machine, and once I have accident. And that's how I meet that boy. Because that boy-- we was like a prisoners, you know-- and to take me to bandage my hand, and getting better, he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is he? I don't know who you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that man-- that boy was a-- a fire-- fireman. But they trust him. And he took me to the doctor and wait for me. I shouldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Escaping. And I was all-- of course, I was crying, because I was shame of myself, like a-- like a criminal that-- I was 18, 19-- 19, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had an accident in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to your hand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I hurt my finger, and coming off a top--

speaker: interviewer
text: The nail?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not the nail, the-- the-- the meat, [LAUGHS] and I still have it, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent you to a hospital or a doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a-- a doctor. And every day, I have to go to the fresh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dressing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Band-aid-- the dressing. And that boy took me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Always the same boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always the same boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was somebody you got to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he can keep his eyes on me to-- I shouldn't escape. And he feel so sorry for me, because I was always crying. And he said, oh, don't cry. Don't cry-- nevermind-- nevermind. And that's how I can escape that time in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you tell us that, Elizabeth, just tell us about where you lived at this time. While you were working in the factory, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a camp-- where is the girls. We live in the double-decker beds. And we was all the young girls together. And the separate place-- not with the-- and when we working, when the bomb coming to the English airplanes and the Americans, they have a bunker where we go. But the Jewish girls have to go separate than the other workers, because we worked together with the other-- the other workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-Jewish workers as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jew, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they working with you or supervising?

speaker: interviewee
text: Supervising.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't work in the same capacity as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: If-- if somebody have no qualification, then she working with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these people under guard like you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when you worked in the cotton factory--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were you under guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were the non-Jewish people under guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but they was free. They can go, but not with the stars. We was together, but we have the stars so that everybody can see it. Because it was that big-- like my hand-- have to-- was a measurement-- how big have to be the star.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you get paid for working in the cotton factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think I get some money. I think I get some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: As much as the non-Jewish worker?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Because they give us the food. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did they give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How can you imagine? And they give us the shelter, you know, to where to-- And you could still go freely in and out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't by this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- not in that stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Although they bring it from the countryside, the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Police.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the very strong police there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, yes, of course-- and

speaker: interviewer
text: And dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: --bayonet, you know the things. And-- and they-- we-- we couldn't go out. And my mother cooking something what was my favorite, and bring it to me in the afternoon-- but she can't come in. She just give me in the fence a little bit of soup and things to-- to have a little bit of home cooking to her little girl. And then one day, she come and said to me, I heard they take us away. I said oh, no. It's impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what-- what they want with the old women and the old mens, you know? Because no young people was anymore in the ghetto. And-- but if I say to her that time to go somewhere, maybe she's still alive, and I never forgive myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to her, no, Mommy. You just go home. Nothing will happen. And I never see her anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say go home, you mean back to her room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to the-- back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take her. Next morning, 6 o'clock, they take her away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I know? My-- my father give some money of that man who was in-- working in the same place where I was working. And he bring me the news. And he said--

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish or a non-Jewish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish, non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he brought you the news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, to--

speaker: interviewer
text: The brick factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brick factory to-- to ready to Auschwitz. And that's why I was escaping, because I want to go after her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We was ready to the wagon in the factory. They come to us in the morning. They said, put your things together, because you're going somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they cut our hair shorter to have it-- you can see everybody can see it. They are a Jewish girls, you know? Then it was a shorter, I had. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I have the star always in my working blouse. And I put it on. And we're going in a line. And they take us to the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in June, early June.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it cold by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's started the hot weather-- spring-- summertime coming. And they take us to the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you to the wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walk-- walk to the-- the wagon, come into the factory. Because that was a big factory. They have their own-- own train line, you know? So it was the-- the train come out, and they put us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the wagon look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Like they take the sheeps, and the-- the things, you know, the animals-- exactly the same wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people do they put in the wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ugh. [SIGHS] You can't count it. We was-- we was like a herrings. But then a [? siren ?] started.

speaker: interviewer
text: The siren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Siren. And they said, everybody out! Everybody out!

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they? Were they German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: German soldiers and Hungarian soldiers. And they said, everybody out and go to the bunker, the-- the Jewish bunker. And I said to myself, no, not me. I run to the non-Jewish bunker, I take off my blouse where I have the star. And I have a scarf. And I put it on my head. And I go to the toilet. And I don't move-- when everybody settled down. And then I leave my parcel there, and I walk out. And I started to run to the gate. And then that boy who was taking me to the doctor's--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the fire engine truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the fire engine. He was standing there and look at me. What-- what-- what-- what did I do over there? And I shake my head. I said, oh, don't. And he turned around. And between the two soldier with the gun, I run out in the gate, and I take a big breath. And I run to my father place. Nobody was in the street, because it-- it was a bomb--

speaker: interviewer
text: Air raid.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is it, yeah-- [NON-ENGLISH], you know? [SIGHS] And I went to my father place. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Elizabeth, before you tell us, did the guards not stop you, wanting to look at papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was oh, so quick! You know, they-- they don't realize that what I doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the gates open?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the gates open, and the two soldiers standing, and all the other soldiers around it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they not running to the bunkers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they can't. They have to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Remain.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the gate, you know? They look after the gate. And even that boy, he don't. Because he have to watch who is going out and who is coming in. And they-- they don't realize it what I doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was so quick. And they just-- a-- a-- a second, and I was out. They even don't-- don't know what's happening. And I turned straight away to the other street, you know? And I was disappearing. Nobody came after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They don't realize it-- I belongs to them-- to they.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: When they are realize it, I was so far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was that from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: How far? [SIGHS] About 20 minutes running. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were buses and trams still running at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Have no-- every-- when was a-- a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Air raid?

speaker: interviewee
text: --air raid, that it's everything stopped-- no moving, no-- no horses, nothing. Everybody stopped. And everybody run to the bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were there lots of people? That--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: No one was in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no, nobody was in the street-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you ran to your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I ran to my father, and when they see me, they get the shock in their life. And [SIGHS] my father said, what-- what you doing here? What you doing here? I said, I run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, but you can't do it. I have my family, my-- my son, my wife. I said, all right, let me sit down and catch my breath, you know? Because I was running. And then-- He didn't want you to stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He said-- then he go to the other room with his wife, and then he come back and said, you can't stay here. I can't risk my family. I said, I am not your family? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He don't answer me. And then the bell ring, because he has a lock-up gate, you know, lock-up piece. And it was that man come to my father whom he give money to tell him what's happening to me. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH]. Now they take the girls away. Your girl is in the wagon. She going to out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he give him again money and said to him, thank you very much. And again, if you heard something, you come. So then he gone. And I said to my father, what you want I should do? He said, you can't stay here. I said, all right. Give me a piece of bread. [LAUGHS] And I know my mother have a friend in-- a lady friend in Rakospalota. That's where your holiday house was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. That's a-- that's a next town--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --like a Bondi and Paddington, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: A suburb.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suburb, yeah. I said to him, I-- I go to her. Maybe she give me--

speaker: interviewer
text: A non-Jewish friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish friend, yeah. So I tell him the address, and there I go. And I went to there. I walk about 1-- 1 hour, 1 and a 1/2 hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody saw you? Nobody stopped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody stopped me, because I was-- have-- you know, that modern way I put that scarf on, you know, to-- like a holiday girl. And I have a sleeveless frock on, you know? Because I haven't got my working things on. And I was a pretty girl. And nobody think I am Jewish. And I-- I walk. And I walk, and I [SIGHS] went to her. And she said to me--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shh-- well, I wanted to-- Speltzer or something like that. I wanted to tell you quickly, and I forgot it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Something asha-- something started.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she say when she saw you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was so surprised. And she said, look. I-- I would like to have you, but the air soldiers in the corners that-- who-- who shot the airplanes, you know, they're here. And they're coming here to have water and things. You can't stay here. I said, all right. But let me sit down. [LAUGHS] And I eat my bread, what my father give me. And I was crying, of course. And I don't know what to do. And then the door was open, and my father wife come after me. She said to me, your father send me. You have to go back to the factory, because they stop the transport. It's not going. So you should go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say, it's no other way. Where shall I go? What shall I do? So I go back. I-- I started to walk with her to go back to the factory. Because-- Did she walk also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she walk also. And so [SIGHS] we don't talk anything. And then when we come in the corner, a pointed corner--

speaker: interviewer
text: Intersection.

speaker: interviewee
text: --section, and that way you go to factory, and that way you go to my father place. And I said to her, you know, I want to see my father again. She said, no. You have to come with me. Your father said to me I take you back. I said, I don't go back. I go and see my father. He is my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was very upset, but she see she can't talk to me. So we went home. OK, thank you.
